metadata.summary: Django coding standards for consistent maintainable code: project structure, service layer, selectors, type hints, import organization, error handling, testing strategies for Django 5.x+ projects.

content.title: Django Coding Standards
content.summary: LLM-optimized patterns for Django 5.x+ based on HackSoft Styleguide and Two Scoops. Covers project structure, service layer architecture, selectors, type hints, import organization that separate concerns and improve testability, maintainability, and consistency across Django applications.

why.p: Consistency in code structure enables faster onboarding, easier code review, and reduces the number of bugs introduced by different developers using different patterns. A standard project structure means models, views, serializers, services, and tests are in predictable locations. Service layers decouple business logic from HTTP concerns, making code testable without mocking requests. Selectors encapsulate complex queries so they're not scattered across views and tests. Type hints prevent whole classes of bugs (None type errors, wrong field types) and make IDE autocomplete reliable. Explicit import organization (avoiding wildcards, using aliases for cross-app imports) prevents circular dependencies and makes dependencies explicit.

list.item: Starting a new Django project that will grow beyond 10K LOC.
Building APIs with multiple endpoints and complex business logic.
Leading a team where consistency matters (CR time, onboarding, handoff).
Projects with tight quality requirements (testability, maintainability, CI gates).
Refactoring legacy Django code that lacks clear structure.
Tiny scripts or one-off CLI tools — overhead exceeds value.
Solo greenfield projects with no intention of team growth.
Strict legacy systems where changing structure would require massive refactor.

section.name: core-principles
section.p: The foundation of Django coding standards rests on several core principles:

list.item: Services for writes: All business logic for write operations goes in service functions, not views.
Selectors for reads: Complex queries go in selector functions, not scattered across views.
Explicit imports: Use aliases for cross-app imports to prevent circular dependencies and make edges explicit.
Type hints everywhere: All function parameters and returns are typed for IDE support and bug prevention.
Constants over magic: Use TextChoices and constants.py instead of string literals or numeric codes.
Validation in models: Use clean() for multi-field validations; serializers validate shape only.

section.name: project-structure
section.p: Standard Django project layout that scales from 10 to 100K+ LOC.

rule.statement: Organize code into apps (domains) following business domains, not layers. Each app has models, services, selectors, apis, serializers, tests. Config and core folders hold shared code (settings, exceptions, pagination, permissions, base models).
rule.rationale: Domain-driven structure makes it easy to find related code. A business domain (orders, users, products) is complete within one app folder. Avoids the "where does this go?" confusion of layer-based structure (all models in models/ folder, all views in views/).

example.title: Recommended project structure
example.code: project/ ├── config/ # Django configuration │ ├── settings/ │ │ ├── base.py # Common settings │ │ ├── development.py # Dev overrides │ │ └── production.py # Prod overrides │ ├── urls.py # Root URL config │ ├── celery.py # Celery app │ ├── wsgi.py # WSGI entry │ └── asgi.py # ASGI entry │ ├── apps/ # Domain applications │ ├── users/ │ │ ├── __init__.py │ │ ├── apps.py │ │ ├── constants.py # TextChoices, limits │ │ ├── models.py # Data structure │ │ ├── services.py # Write logic │ │ ├── selectors.py # Read logic │ │ ├── apis.py # HTTP views │ │ ├── serializers.py # DRF serializers │ │ ├── urls.py # URL patterns │ │ ├── admin.py # Admin config │ │ ├── permissions.py # Custom permissions │ │ └── tests/ │ │ ├── test_models.py │ │ ├── test_services.py │ │ ├── test_selectors.py │ │ └── test_apis.py │ │ │ ├── orders/ │ │ ├── models.py │ │ ├── services.py │ │ ├── selectors.py │ │ ├── apis.py │ │ └── tests/ │ │ │ └── products/ │ ├── models.py │ ├── services.py │ └── ... │ ├── core/ # Shared code │ ├── models.py # BaseModel │ ├── exceptions.py # Custom exceptions │ ├── pagination.py # Custom pagination │ ├── permissions.py # Custom permissions │ └── utils.py # Utilities │ ├── tests/ │ ├── conftest.py # pytest fixtures │ └── factories/ # Factory Boy factories │ ├── manage.py ├── pyproject.toml # Dependencies + tools └── requirements/ ├── base.txt ├── development.txt └── production.txt

section.name: naming-conventions
section.p: Consistent naming makes code predictable and searchable.

rule.statement: Services: use <entity>_<action> pattern (user_create, order_process, product_update). Selectors: use <entity>_<query> pattern (user_list, order_get_by_id). Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. Models: PascalCase, singular. Apps: snake_case, plural.
rule.rationale: Consistent naming makes it obvious where to find code. A grep for "user_create" finds all create services for users. Naming patterns make intent clear: user_list is a query (selector), order_process is a write operation (service).

example.title: Service and selector naming
example.code:
# services.py — naming: <entity>_<action> def user_create(*, email: str, name: str) -> User: """Create a new user.""" pass
def order_process_payment(*, order: Order, payment_method: str) -> PaymentResult: """Process payment for an order.""" pass
def user_send_verification_email(*, user: User) -> None: """Send verification email to user.""" pass
# selectors.py — naming: <entity>_<query> def user_list(*, is_active: bool = True) -> QuerySet[User]: """Get all active users.""" pass
def order_list_for_user(*, user: User) -> QuerySet[Order]: """Get all orders for a user.""" pass
def order_get_by_id(*, order_id: int) -> Order: """Get a single order by ID.""" pass
# constants.py class OrderStatus(models.TextChoices): PENDING = 'pending', 'Pending' PROCESSING = 'processing', 'Processing' COMPLETED = 'completed', 'Completed'
MAX_ORDER_ITEMS = 1000 DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE = 20

section.name: import-organization
section.p: Organized imports prevent circular dependencies and make code dependencies explicit.

rule.statement: Organize imports in order: __future__, standard library, third-party, Django, first-party, local. Use aliases (as) for cross-app imports. Use TYPE_CHECKING block for imports that would cause circular dependencies.
rule.rationale: This order is enforced by isort with Django profile. Aliases make cross-app edges explicit; they're searchable. TYPE_CHECKING prevents circular import errors while preserving type hints.

example.title: Proper import organization
example.code:
# 1. Future imports from __future__ import annotations
# 2. Standard library import uuid from datetime import datetime, timedelta from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
# 3. Third-party from django.db import models, transaction from django.utils import timezone from rest_framework import serializers, status from rest_framework.views import APIView
# 4. Cross-app imports (ALWAYS use alias) from apps.orders import models as order_models from apps.orders import services as order_services from apps.users import selectors as user_selectors
# 5. Local imports (relative) from .constants import OrderStatus, MAX_ITEMS from .models import Order from .serializers import OrderCreateRequest, OrderResponse
# 6. TYPE_CHECKING imports (avoid circular imports) if TYPE_CHECKING: from apps.users.models import User

section.name: service-layer-pattern
section.p: Service layer encapsulates write operations and business logic, keeping views thin and logic testable.

rule.statement: All write operations (create, update, delete, side effects) go in service functions. Services take keyword-only arguments, use transaction.atomic() for multi-model writes, call full_clean() before save(), and raise domain exceptions (not HTTP exceptions).
rule.rationale: Services are independently testable without HTTP request mocks. Keyword-only args prevent accidental positional args. Transactions ensure atomicity. full_clean() catches model-level validation errors before DB save. Domain exceptions decouple business logic from HTTP status codes.

example.title: Service function with proper patterns
example.code:
# apps/orders/services.py from django.db import transaction
from apps.orders.models import Order, OrderItem from apps.products import selectors as product_selectors
def order_create( *, user: "User", items: list[dict], shipping_address: str, ) -> Order: """ Create a new order with items.
Args: user: The user placing the order items: List of {"product_id": int, "quantity": int} shipping_address: Delivery address
Returns: Created Order instance
Raises: ValidationError: If any product is unavailable """ # Validate products exist and have stock product_ids = [item["product_id"] for item in items] products = product_selectors.product_list_by_ids(product_ids=product_ids)
if len(products) != len(product_ids): raise ValidationError("Some products not found")
with transaction.atomic(): order = Order( user=user, shipping_address=shipping_address, status=OrderStatus.PENDING, ) order.full_clean() order.save()
for item in items: product = next(p for p in products if p.id == item["product_id"]) OrderItem.objects.create( order=order, product=product, quantity=item["quantity"], unit_price=product.price, )
order.calculate_total()
return order

section.name: selector-pattern
section.p: Selectors encapsulate complex queries so they're not repeated across views, tests, and management commands.

rule.statement: All complex read operations (filters, aggregations, prefetches) go in selector functions. Selectors return QuerySet (for chainable queries) or single objects. Selectors include prefetch_related and select_related to prevent N+1 queries.
rule.rationale: Selectors centralize query logic, making optimization easier. A change to a selector (adding an index, prefetch) automatically propagates to all uses. QuerySet returns allow chaining; single-object returns are cached and fast.

example.title: Selector functions with optimization

section.name: error-handling
section.p: Custom exception hierarchy makes error handling explicit and decouples business logic from HTTP status codes.

rule.statement: Define custom exception classes inheriting from ApplicationError. Services raise domain exceptions. Views catch exceptions and map to HTTP status codes (404 NotFound → 404_NOT_FOUND, ValidationError → 400_BAD_REQUEST).
rule.rationale: Domain exceptions don't know about HTTP; they represent business failures. Views map them to HTTP status codes. This decoupling makes services reusable in async tasks, CLI commands, and other entry points.

example.title: Exception hierarchy and handling

section.name: testing-strategy
section.p: Test organization mirrors code structure: test_models, test_services, test_selectors, test_apis.

rule.statement: Test structure mirrors code: test_models.py tests model validation and properties, test_services.py tests business logic with database (no mocks), test_selectors.py tests queries for N+1, test_apis.py tests HTTP with test client.
rule.rationale: This structure makes it obvious what to test. Model tests don't call services. Service tests don't mock models (use real database). Selector tests verify optimization (assert prefetch_related works). API tests verify HTTP layer including auth and status codes.

rule.statement: Use Factory Boy for test data. Create factories/ folder with factories for each model. Use SubFactory for relationships. Avoid fixtures; factories are clearer and more composable.
rule.rationale: Factory Boy tests are readable (OrderFactory(items=2) creates order with 2 items) and DRY. Factories compose easily; you can override any field in tests. Fixtures are a tangled global state.

example.title: Factory pattern for test data
example.code:
# tests/factories/orders.py import factory from factory.django import DjangoModelFactory
from apps.orders.models import Order, OrderItem from apps.orders.constants import OrderStatus
class OrderFactory(DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = Order
user = factory.SubFactory("tests.factories.users.UserFactory") status = OrderStatus.PENDING shipping_address = factory.Faker("address") total = factory.Faker("pydecimal", left_digits=4, right_digits=2)
class OrderItemFactory(DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = OrderItem
order = factory.SubFactory(OrderFactory) product = factory.SubFactory("tests.factories.products.ProductFactory") quantity = factory.Faker("random_int", min=1, max=10) unit_price = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda obj: obj.product.price)
# usage in tests def test_order_creation(): order = OrderFactory(status=OrderStatus.COMPLETED, items=3) assert order.items.count() == 3

section.name: type-hints
section.p: Type hints prevent bugs, enable IDE autocomplete, and serve as inline documentation.

rule.statement: All function parameters and return types are annotated. Use QuerySet[Model] for queryset returns. Use | None instead of Optional. Use modern syntax (list[str] instead of List[str]). Add TYPE_CHECKING imports for forward references.
rule.rationale: Type hints catch errors at development time (wrong field type, None handling). IDEs autocomplete properly with hints. Models should not be imported at module level if it causes circular imports; use TYPE_CHECKING and string quotes for forward references.

example.title: Type hints in services and selectors

section.name: django-6-features
section.p: Django 6.x adds native background tasks and improved template partials.

rule.statement: Use Django 6.x native @task decorator for background jobs instead of Celery when possible. Tasks are simpler, have no external dependency, and integrate with Django's task scheduler.
rule.rationale: Django tasks are suitable for most workloads (send email, update cache, report). Celery is still better for complex workflows, scheduling, and monitoring. Native tasks have lower operational overhead.

example.title: Native Django 6.x background task
example.code:
# config/tasks.py from django.tasks import task
@task def send_order_confirmation(order_id: int) -> None: """Send order confirmation email asynchronously.""" from apps.orders import selectors as order_selectors from apps.notifications import services as notification_services
order = order_selectors.order_get_by_id(order_id=order_id) notification_services.send_email( to=order.user.email, template="order_confirmation", context={"order": order}, )
# Usage in service def order_complete(*, order: Order) -> Order: order.status = OrderStatus.COMPLETED order.save(update_fields=["status", "updated_at"])
# Queue background task send_order_confirmation.enqueue(order_id=order.id)
return order

section.name: code-review-checklist
section.p: Points to verify in code review to ensure standards are maintained.

checklist.check: Business logic is in services, not views/serializers.
Complex queries are in selectors, not views.
Services use transaction.atomic() for multi-model writes.
Services raise domain exceptions, not HTTP exceptions.
Services take keyword-only arguments (no positional args after *).
All function parameters have type hints.
All function return types are annotated.
No circular imports (use TYPE_CHECKING for forward refs).
Cross-app imports use aliases (from apps.X import Y as X_Y).
No string literals for statuses/enums (use TextChoices constants).
Models use clean() for multi-field validation, not save().
No business logic in serializer validate_* methods.
No business logic in model save() override.
Tests use Factory Boy, not fixtures.
Test APIs with Django test client (self.client.get/post), not view directly.
No DEBUG=True, SECRET_KEY leaks, or credentials in code.

template.purpose: Code templates for services, selectors, models, tests.
